key_insight: Optimization can be viewed as a coupled geometric system where changing the metric can improve conditioning and transport, but cannot eliminate intrinsic topological or Morse-theoretic obstructions.
engineering_relevance: The framework gives optimizer designers a benchmark for what adaptive preconditioning or metric learning can and cannot achieve, helping separate real gains from changes in geometry alone.
method: Define optimization as joint evolution of parameters, transported particles, and a time-varying Riemannian metric.
Use invariant observables to separate fixed obstructions from improvable geometric mismatch.

Characterize dynamic geometric complexity as the minimum geometric cost needed to reduce a chosen difficulty measure; in the quadratic oracle case, it equals an affine-invariant log-spectrum distance to well-conditioned metrics.
evidence_signal: The paper is theory-only and validates claims through formal definitions, invariance results, and proofs rather than empirical experiments or benchmark data.
watch_item: Practical use depends on specifying admissible metric families, curvature estimates, and discretization errors, so implementable gains may be limited by approximation quality.
paper_title: Optimization Geometrodynamics: A Framework for Dynamic Geometric Optimization
